metadata.title: Research Hot, Cold, and Excluded Tumors with Claude: Why Immunotherapy Works in Some Cancers

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. The answer is whether the tumor has T cells inside it? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The answer is whether the tumor has T cells inside it?
props.segment: Research Hot, Cold, and Excluded Tumors

beats.narration_text: Pembrolizumab produces 5-year survival above 50% in metastatic melanoma. In pancreatic cancer, the response rate is near zero. Same drug, same mechanism, completely different diseases. The answer is whether the tumor has T cells inside it.

Three tumor immune phenotypes explain the immunotherapy response spectrum. Hot inflamed tumors — high TIL density, high mutational burden, PD-L1 expression — respond to checkpoint blockade: the brake is on existing T cells. Cold desert tumors — no T cell infiltration, immune exclusion — fail because releasing a brake on absent T cells does nothing. Excluded tumors — T cells at the periphery, stromal barrier preventing entry — represent a third biology: immune response initiated but physically blocked from reaching cancer cells.
Ask Claude to classify tumor immune phenotypes.

props.command: claude "Research the hot, cold, and excluded tumor immune phenotypes as predictors of checkpoint inhibitor response. For each phenotype: the molecular definition, representative cancer types, checkpoint inhibitor response rates, and strategies being tested to convert cold or excluded tumors to hot. Include data from KEYNOTE-006 melanoma, pancreatic cancer failures, and MSI-H as the clearest hot-tumor biomarker."

props.code:
# tumor_immune_phenotypes_brief.md  --  Claude output

## HOT (inflamed)
- Markers: high TIL, high TMB, MSI-H
- Examples: melanoma, dMMR colorectal
- Response: KEYNOTE-006 — 5-year OS >50%
- Intervention: checkpoint blockade works

## COLD (desert)
- Markers: no TIL, immune ignorance
- Examples: pancreatic cancer, GBM
- Response rate: <5%
- Intervention: STING agonists, radiation, neoantigen vaccines

## EXCLUDED
- Markers: TIL at margins, desmoplastic stroma
- Examples: pancreatic, ovarian
- Intervention: anti-fibrotic + checkpoint combo, CXCR4 inhibitors

beats.narration_text: Classification brief generated: KEYNOTE-006 5-year melanoma OS confirmed above 50%, versus historical median of 9 months. Microsatellite-stable pancreatic cancer checkpoint response: less than 3% ORR in multiple trials. MSI-H correctly identified as clearest biomarker — hypermutation drives neoantigen load and T cell recognition. Excluded phenotype in pancreatic cancer linked to CAF-mediated stromal barrier with desmoplasia. Conversion strategies: STING agonists trigger innate immune activation in cold tumors; high-dose radiation creates in situ vaccination; CXCR4 inhibitors improve T cell trafficking in excluded tumors.

props.command: claude "Why are TMB and PD-L1 expression imperfect predictors of checkpoint inhibitor response? What additional biomarkers are under investigation — including tumor inflammation signature, spatial immune mapping, and TCR clonality?"

beats.narration_text: Biomarker limitation analysis: TMB predicts response in some cancers but not others. Reason: neoantigen quality matters as much as quantity — a high-TMB tumor can have many non-immunogenic neoantigens. PD-L1 expression is dynamic, varies between primary and metastasis, and four different PD-L1 IHC assays exist with different cutoffs. Additional biomarkers under investigation: tumor inflammation signature — an 18-gene panel; spatial immune mapping measuring proximity of T cells to cancer cells, not just density; TCR clonality — oligoclonal T cell expansion predicts response better than total TIL count.

The checkpoint inhibitor releases a brake. If there are no T cells in the tumor, releasing the brake on non-existent T cells does nothing. The biology of cold tumors is not a drug problem — it is an immune desert problem. Converting the desert to an inflamed tumor is the unsolved second act of immuno-oncology. The phenotype classification maps the problem; the conversion strategy is where the next decade of trials lives.
Next: research why less than 15% of cells in a pancreatic tumor are actually cancer — and why that makes it the hardest cancer to treat.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Hot, Cold, and Excluded Tumors with Claude: Why Immunotherapy] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
